coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from cumin (Cuminum cyminum) seeds have been synthesized using the hydrothermal technique and were characterized by HR-TEM, XPS, FTIR, RAMAN, UV–Vis, and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The photostability of CQDs was investigated through various tests such as in high saline conditions, with their storage time, under UV and white light exposure, pH values, and different solvents. They were found selective and sensitive to Cr6+ ions, so their potential as a fluorescent probe for Cr6+ ions sensing is being studied. The Lowest limit of detection (LOD) value for the current ion sensor in an aqueous solution is 8.3 μM. The temperature dependence of PL intensity has also been utilized to demonstrate a temperature sensor with a thermal sensitivity of 0.658 °C-1. This study contributes significantly to temperature sensing and metal ions detection technologies by utilizing CQDs synthesized from natural precursors.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly carbon quantum dots as a bi-functional fluorescent probe for Cr (VI) detection and optical thermometry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from cumin (Cuminum cyminum) seeds have been synthesized using the hydrothermal technique and were characterized by HR-TEM, XPS, FTIR, RAMAN, UV–Vis, and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The photostability of CQDs was investigated through various tests such as in high saline conditions, with their storage time, under UV and white light exposure, pH values, and different solvents. They were found selective and sensitive to Cr6+ ions, so their potential as a fluorescent probe for Cr6+ ions sensing is being studied. The Lowest limit of detection (LOD) value for the current ion sensor in an aqueous solution is 8.3 μM. The temperature dependence of PL intensity has also been utilized to demonstrate a temperature sensor with a thermal sensitivity of 0.658 °C-1. This study contributes significantly to temperature sensing and metal ions detection technologies by utilizing CQDs synthesized from natural precursors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Materials composed of carbon, having sizes <10 nm are called CQDs. These nanomaterials possess unique properties because of quantum confinement effects, and their reactivity, solubility, and stability are generally influenced by surface functional groups like amino (-NH2), carboxyl (-COOH), carbonyl (-C=O), hydroxyl (-OH), etc. Owing to their outstanding properties such as low-cost environmentally friendly production, biocompatibility, and unique optical characteristics, they find applications in various domains like sensors, bioimaging, optoelectronics, photocatalysis, environmental monitoring, drug delivery, etc. [1–5]. CQDs synthesis provides a variety of options for precursors and preparation methods, such as plant-based sources, byproducts from agriculture, biomass, natural polymers, extracts from nature, organic waste, and animal-derived materials [6–24], with preparation techniques like hydrothermal/solvothermal, microwave-assisted, chemical vapor deposition, chemical oxidation, sol-gel, laser ablation, sonochemical, ultrasonic methods, flame synthesis, etc. Among these, the hydrothermal method has gained significant interest owing to its simplicity, scalability, reduced cost, versatility, efficient yield and quality, eco-friendliness, etc. [25–34].

CQDs with their distinct optical and electronic characteristics have significant potential for temperature and metal ions sensing. The change in the PL intensity, often due to quenching or enhancement serves as a valuable parameter for these applications. PL quenching being highly sensitive to environmental changes enables effective detection of analytes. Metal ions can attenuate PL intensity via the formation of complexes, static quenching, and dynamic quenching mechanisms like the Inner Filter Effect (IFE), Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), and Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) process [35]. Temperature-induced quenching results from elevated non-radiative recombination rates at high temperatures. Despite their promising feature, the key challenges in the field like enhancing their selectivity, sensitivity, reproducibility, and stability under varying environmental conditions persist. Addressing these challenges could significantly enhance the performance and broaden the practical applications of CQDs in sensing technologies.

Among metal ions, hexavalent chromium (Cr6+) is of great concern due to its high toxicity, carcinogenicity, and as a major environmental pollutant. Industrial activities such as electroplating, leather tanning, and metal processing contribute to Cr⁶⁺ contamination in water and soil. Due to its high solubility and mobility, Cr⁶⁺ poses severe health risks, including cancer and organ damage. Thus, sensitive and selective detection of Cr6+ ions is crucial for environmental safety and regulatory compliance. While many CQDs-based sensors have been developed for Cr6+ detection many suffer from low selectivity, sensitivity, and instability in complex environmental samples like groundwater and tap water. Some of the CQDs-based sensors for Cr6+ detection and temperature sensing are listed in Table 1 and Table 3.

This study presents a novel dual-function sensor that combines Cr6+ detection and temperature sensing using CQDs. A major innovation of this work is the use of a novel natural precursor (cumin seeds) which has not been explored in previous studies for these applications. This precursor not only enhances the fluorescence properties of the CQDs but also provides an eco-friendly and cost-effective approach to synthesis. The sensor demonstrates sensitivity and selectivity for Cr⁶⁺ ions, even in the presence of interfering ions, which makes it ideal for real-world environmental applications like tap water and groundwater monitoring. In addition to Cr⁶⁺ sensing, the same CQD-based sensor demonstrates a high thermal sensitivity of 0.658 % °C-1, which surpasses the performance of most sensors reported in the literature. This dual-sensing capability (simultaneously detecting Cr⁶⁺ and monitoring temperature) sets this sensor apart, enabling it to serve multiple purposes in environmental monitoring and industrial applications where both metal ions detection and temperature are critical. Its ability to perform reliably in complex, real-world environments (such as tap water and groundwater) and under varying conditions (like temperature fluctuations and light exposure) ensures its practical applicability and scalability for widespread use in environmental monitoring, industrial waste management, and public health protection.

Cumin seeds (Cuminum cyminum) were taken from the hostel mess at the National Institute of Technology in Hamirpur, India. calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), cadmium chloride (CdCl2.H2O), nickel chloride (NiCl2·6H2O), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), copper acetate ((CH3COO)2Cu.H2O), stannous chloride (SnCl2·2H2O), cobalt (2) chloride (CoCl2·6H2O), sodium dichromate (Na2Cr2O7·2H2O), titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), sodium arsenate (Na2HAsO4·7H2O) acetone ((CH3)2CO), quinine sulfate ((C20H24N2O2)2.H2SO4·2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), Deionized water (DI), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium arsenate (Na2HAsO4·7H2O), ammonium sulfate ((NH4)2.SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and mercuric chloride (HgCl2). The purity level of the used chemicals is ≥ 98 %.

Cumin seeds were subjected to drying in a hot air oven for 24 h before being pulverized into a fine powder. The fine powder of cumin seeds (0.2 g) was mixed with 50 ml of DI and processed using ultrasonication for 1 hour. Subsequently, the solution is moved to an autoclave and treated at 215 °C for 5 h. Once the reactor comes to normal temperature, the solution is centrifuged and syringe-filtered before its utilization. A visual overview of the synthesis process is presented in Fig. 1 .

The photoluminescence characteristics were examined on the Shimadzu RF-6000 Spectrofluorophotometer, which was furnished with a Xenon lamp. UV–visible spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer UV-2450 spectrophotometer. For FTIR analysis, a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 65 spectrometer was employed. XPS was performed with a Thermo Fisher Scientific Nexsa base system. HR-TEM imaging was carried out using an F.E.I. Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN instrument from the USA. To assess photostability, a compact fluorescent bulb (CFL)/white light (make: PHILIPS, 8 W, 220–240 V, 50–60 Hz, 6500 K, 400 lm) and a UV lamp (Make: Bio-World, Wavelengths: 254 and 365 nm, UV viewing area: 10 × 5 cm, Operating voltage: 230 V, frequency: 50/60 Hz) were used. The sample temperature was controlled by a water bath connected to the spectrofluorophotometer during measurements.

We assessed the photostability of the prepared CQDs by testing under different storage times, high salt levels, multiple solvents, UV light, fluorescent light, and a variety of pH levels. For storage time, they were stored for approximately two months (60 days) in cool and dark conditions and the PL measurements were taken every 10 days. To test the impact of high salt concentrations on PL intensity, different concentrations of NaCl are prepared in DI. The solutions were illuminated by a UV lamp (wavelength 254 nm), and fluorescent light for 180 min each, with PL intensity measured every 30 min. To study the effects of pH values and solvents, CQDs were solubilized in diverse pH conditions (1–14) and solvents such as DI, acetone, methanol, and ethanol. A small amount of the CQDs was directly mixed with each solvent under ambient conditions without additional modifications. To create solutions of different pH values, NaOH and HCl were used. All the tests mentioned above were conducted at ambient temperature, and the measurements were taken at 371 nm excitation.

A Spectro fluorophotometer with an integrated water bath was used to adjust the temperature and measure the PL intensity of the samples at various temperatures. Temperatures were selected within the range of 14 °C to 98 °C, with an increment of 14 °C. The samples were allowed to equilibrate at each temperature for 7 min before measurements were taken.

Ca2+, Ag2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Pb2+, K+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Ti4+, Zn2+ and, As5+ bulk solutions with a 5 mM concentration were prepared in DI. The blank sample was prepared by adding CQDs (200 µL) to DI (2.5 ml). The sample solutions were made by mixing 200 µL of CQDs into 2.5 ml of the earlier prepared bulk solutions and left for 5 min before taking the measurements. The PL intensity of sample solutions was normalized to the PL intensity of blank solution. The sensitivity of Cr6+ ions was elucidated in various concentrations of Cr6+ (0–500 µM) in DI. Similar to the process discussed above 200 µL of CQDs were added to 2.5 ml solutions of different Cr6+ ions concentrations. The PL intensity was monitored at 371 nm excitation wavelength at normal temperature conditions.

The subsequent equation was employed to compute the LOD value.

HRTEM analysis is a powerful technique for acquiring detailed structural information about CQDs. It offers insights into their size, shape, crystallinity, etc. Fig. 2 a-d showcases HRTEM images of CQDs at different resolutions i.e. 100, 50, 20, and 5 nm (inset 500pm), respectively. The images depict uniformly distributed CQDs with a nearly spherical shape. Their sizes are estimated using ImageJ software. They vary in size from 0 to 8 nm, with the dominant fraction lying between the 2 to 4 nm range (Fig. 2f). On average, CQDs exhibit a size of 3.4 nm. In the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) micrograph (Fig. 2e), diffused concentric rings without any bright spots reflect a lack of long-range order or crystallinity in CQDs. This reveals that the CQDs likely exhibit an amorphous or disordered structure rather than a crystalline one. The lack of well-defined diffraction spots commonly associated with crystalline materials shows that the carbon atoms in CQDs are distributed randomly or in short-range order rather than in a regular lattice structure [36,37]. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) yields useful information about the elemental makeup, chemical state, and surface functional groups of materials like CQDs. Full-scan XPS spectrum of presently synthesized CQDs is shown in Fig. 3 a. Four major peaks at binding energies 285.45, 400.88, 532.79, and 687.95 eV were observed which are ascribed to C1 s, N1 s, O1 s, and F1 s respectively [38]. It is reasonable to infer that CQDs contain carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine. The atomic weight % is as follows: carbon (62.37 %), nitrogen (0.26 %), oxygen (10.35 %), and fluorine (26.66 %) (Fig. 3b). Based on the percentage obtained from the analysis carbon is the dominant component of CQDs. The obtained peaks from XPS analysis are further deconvoluted to get deeper insights into the chemical environment and specific functional groups associated with the elements present in the material. The deconvoluted C1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) shows spikes at 283.8, 285.12, 286.52, 289.07, and 291.01 eV associated with CC, COH, CO, C=O, and C-F, respectively [39–41]. N1 s deconvolution (Fig. 3d) shows two major peaks at 399.4 and 400.5 eV which are due to pyrrolic N and CN groups respectively [42,43]. Peaks at 532.09, 532.74, and 533.87 eV because of OC=O, CO-C/COH, and C=O were observed in the O1 s deconvolution (Fig. 3e) [38,44]. Covalent C-F bonds in the CQDs sample are highlighted by a peak at 688.08 eV (Fig. 3f) [45,46]. XPS analysis has shown that CQDs are predominantly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine, with carbon being the major element. They also incorporate several functional groups related to these constituent elements.

FTIR spectroscopy is a key tool for identifying the bond types and interactions of carbon atoms in CQDs, thereby facilitating the understanding of their chemical structure. In the FTIR spectral profile of CQDs (Fig. 4 a), the dips at 3200–3400 cm-1, 2394 cm-1, 2117 cm-1, 1640 cm-1, and 1279 cm-1 were observed which are related to the OH/NH, O=C=O, C=C=O, C=O/CN, and C-F, groups respectively. It signifies that CQDs contain different chemical moieties on their surface. They significantly impact the performance of CQDs by modifying their stability, solubility across solvents, optical emission and absorption, reactivity, and electronic characteristics. They also facilitate surface modifications that are crucial for various applications.

Raman analysis is an effective tool to probe the molecular structure, crystalline or amorphous nature, and provide detailed information about the vibrational and rotational modes. Analyzing the characteristic D and G bands offers insights into the quality, purity, and structural properties of CQDs. The current sample's Raman analysis (Fig. 4b) revealed peaks at 1387.04 cm⁻¹ and 1578.16 cm⁻¹, which are connected to the D and G bands, respectively. The origin of the G-band lies in the in-plane vibrational activity of sp² hybridized carbon atoms found in graphitic or graphene-like materials, while the defects and disorder in the carbon lattice of graphitic material give rise to the D-band. When comparing the two bands, the D-band is broader and less intense which may suggest a range of disorders and defects within the material but with a lower overall defect density. The G-band is narrow and more intense indicating a well-ordered graphitic structure with a high concentration of sp² hybridized carbon atoms. The amount of disorder in a material is estimated by the intensity ratio of the D-band (ID) to the G-band (IG). In this case, ID = 214.879 and IG = 245.139. So, ID/ IG = 0.876. The value is close but <1, suggesting a moderately disordered structure [47,48]. It reflects a balance between well-ordered graphitic structure and disorders and defects in the material, which means that the material is not purely ordered not purely disordered but instead a mixture of both ordered and disordered states. With a moderate ID/ IG ​ ratio, the material is expected to perform well in applications that demand a balance of electrical conductivity and structural integrity, such as in electrodes, sensors, and various electronic devices.

The energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDS) analysis as indicated in Fig. 4(c) marked the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in the CQDs sample. A comparison of full scan XPS (Fig. 3a) and EDS analysis showed the absence of fluorine in the EDS analysis, while in the XPS study fluorine is present in a good amount (26.66 %). XPS is a surface-sensitive technique involving the detection of photoelectrons from a few (1–10) nanometers of a material surface, while EDS is a bulk-sensitive technique because it involves the detection of X-rays from a large volume of the sample. It may be possible that fluorine is likely to present only on the surface of the sample and not in a significant quantity inside the core.

Gaining insights into the optical properties of CQDs enables us to harness their capabilities across diverse fields from material science and sensing to bioimaging and drug delivery. So a detailed analysis of the optical characteristics of CQDs has been done.

The UV-visible spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 5 a) exhibits significant absorbance in the UV range, with a gradual extension to the visible region, indicating substantial conjugation within the CQDs. Two distinct peaks at around 255 nm and 357 nm are related to π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions [49,50]. In the carbon core's electronic structure, delocalized π electrons facilitate the π to π* transition. Light absorption prompts these electrons to transition from the π bonding orbital to the π antibonding orbital. In the n to π* transition, non-bonding electrons associated with surface functional groups are excited. This results in these electrons moving to the π antibonding orbital of the carbon core, which contributes to UV–Vis absorption. Fig. 5b shows the PL excitation and emission spectra of CQDs. The excitation spectrum ranges from approximately 300–450 nm i.e. in the UV-visible region with a maximum at 371 nm, while the emission is from 350 to 600 nm i.e. primarily in the visible region with maxima at 442 nm. It's noteworthy that the PL excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs are broad, lacking specific characteristic peaks. Consequently, these CQDs can be excited across a wavelength range of 300–450 nm and will emit light across a range of 350–600 nm, depending on the excitation wavelength. Fig. 5c depicts that the CQDs exhibit a range of emission colors from blue to green, yellow, and red. The intensity of the emitted light is dependent on the excitation wavelength, with the blue color showing the maximum intensity observed in this instance. We conducted a detailed examination of the excitation-induced emission properties, and the findings are illustrated in Fig. 5d. Different wavelengths (300, 320, 340, 360, 370, 380, 400, and 420 nm) were used to excite the samples, covering the full range of excitation, and the resulting PL emission spectra were documented. With a shift to a longer excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 360 nm (in 20 nm steps), the PL intensity grows, reaches its peak at 370 nm, and subsequently diminishes from 370 nm to 420 nm. Alongside this, the λmax, which represents the wavelength of maximum PL intensity, shifted from 425 nm to 490 nm, as recorded in Fig. 5f. In Fig. 5e, the integrated PL intensity is plotted against different excitation wavelengths. The intensity initially increases to a peak at 370 nm and subsequently decreases. This indicates that CQDs can emit colors based on the chosen excitation wavelength [51,52]. The CIE plot in Fig. 5g provides additional confirmation, displaying the x and y color coordinates of PL emission spectra across various excitation wavelengths. The plot reveals a shift in color coordinates from blue to green as the excitation wavelength varies from 300 nm to 420 nm. The broad excitation/emission spectra and excitation-induced emission could be possibly due to the non-uniform particle size, quantum confinements, surface states, and functionalization. It is evident from HRTEM analysis that CQDs have a range of sizes (0–8 nm, average = 3.4 nm). Small-sized quantum dots have larger band gaps because of the quantum confinement of the dimensions. So, different-sized CQDs have distinct band gaps. Size variations in CQDs lead to a range of excitation energy levels, which in turn broadens the excitation spectrum. Different-sized CQDs absorb light at different wavelengths, producing a wide excitation band. Similarly, the emission spectrum is broad because different sizes of CQDs emit light at different wavelengths. When excited, CQDs of varying sizes will emit light over a range of wavelengths, leading to a broad emission spectrum. The overlap of emission spectra from various sizes of CQDs contributes to the overall broad emission observed. The aforementioned reason is also valid for the excitation-induced emission spectrum. CQDs respond to excitation and emit light only if their energy gap is in resonance with the excitation wavelength. By varying the excitation wavelength, different CQDs will become excited and emit according to their energy gaps, while others that do not match the new wavelength will remain inactive. This phenomenon leads to the different PL emission spectra corresponding to different excitation wavelengths. In addition to this, surface states and functionalization also contribute to the broad spectra of excitation/emission and excitation-induced emission in CQDs by introducing extra energy levels and altering the electronic properties. Surface states create localized energy levels that increase the range of energy transitions, while functionalization changes the surface chemistry, thereby affecting the CQDs energy levels and optical properties [50,53–58].

For practical applications of CQDs, investigation of their photostability is of utmost importance. The photostability of CQDs underpins their reliability, performance, biocompatibility, safety, and potential for successful product commercialization. Several factors like pH, temperature, ionic strength, light exposure, solvent polarity, oxidative stress, etc. can influence the photostability of CQDs. The study examined CQDs photostability by evaluating changes in PL intensity under various practical conditions, including storage, high salinity, UV/fluorescent light, pH, and different solvents.

The storage stability of CQDs was examined by storing them for 60 days, with the PL intensity being measured at 10-day intervals (Fig. 6 a), where normalized PL intensity is plotted against their storage time). Throughout this period, CQDs show no appreciable change in the PL intensity indicating their resistance to degradation over time. This outcome assures that they retain their optical characteristics for long intervals of time making them suitable for prolonged use in various applications. NaCl is the most common and easily available salt used to investigate the PL behavior in high salt conditions (Fig. 6b, c). Different concentrations of NaCl (0–2.5 M) were prepared in DI and a suitable amount of CQDs solution was added to it. The mixture was allowed to react and the PL measurements were taken after 0, 10, 20, and 30 min. Minor fluctuations in the PL intensity of CQDs were observed which signifies that salty conditions do not significantly affect the PL behavior. The investigation of the stability of CQDs in fluorescent light/UV light is important to ensure their longevity, resilience, and error-free use in many applications like LEDs, displays, solar cells, drug delivery, bioimaging, etc. Given that these applications require extended exposure to fluorescent light /UV light, assessing their stability under such conditions is essential. CQDs were independently exposed to a compact fluorescent lamp (CFL) (Fig. 6d) and a UV lamp (254 nm wavelength, Fig. 6e) for three hours with the recording of PL intensity every 30 min. A minor maximum decrease of 0.04 under CFL and 0.06 under UV light was observed, which suggests excellent stability of CQDs in both lighting conditions, making them an excellent candidate for the aforementioned applications.

Furthermore, different pH environments from highly acidic (pH=1) to highly basic (pH=14) were used to examine the PL behavior of CQDs in these environments. The results (Fig. 6f) show that PL intensity is minimum in highly acidic (pH=1,3) and basic (pH=10,14) environments while it is maximum around neutral pH regions (pH=6,8,9). The observed pH dependence suggests a strong correlation between the surface chemistry of CQDs and their optical properties. As it is evident from the XPS and FTIR analysis there are multiple functional groups on CQDs surfaces. On one hand, in high-pH environments, they can get protonated because of an excess of H+ ions resulting in non-radiative transitions. On the other hand, the deprotonation of these functional groups may take place in highly basic environments. Deprotonated groups may facilitate non-radiative transitions by introducing additional pathways for electron-hole recombination that do not result in photon emission, which ultimately leads to a decrease in PL intensity. In neutral pH regions, a balanced charge distribution on the CQD surface might optimize the conditions for radiative recombination, leading to higher PL intensity [59]. In addition to this, the solubility of CQDs was inspected in solvents like DI, acetone, ethanol, and methanol. They are found soluble in these solvents indicating their hydrophilic nature. The PL intensity is slightly different in each solvent (Fig. 6g), which points out that their electronic structure is sensitive to the solvent environment. The solubility of CQDs in these solvents may be attributed to the presence of numerous hydrophilic groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, amine, etc. on their surface. These groups can form hydrogen bonds or dipole-dipole interactions with the solvent molecules.

Accurate temperature measurements are essential in a wide array of fields and applications, serving as a cornerstone for safety, quality assurance, energy efficiency, and the advancement of scientific knowledge. The distinctive optical and electronic properties of CQDs make them pivotal in temperature sensing applications, as they demonstrate remarkable sensitivity to temperature variations, thereby acting as invaluable assets for temperature monitoring. The PL behavior of CQDs was studied at temperatures from 14 °C to 98 °C with a gap of 14 °C (Fig. 7 a). A decline in the PL intensity was noted as the temperature increased. It remained at approximately 47 % at 98 °C of its starting value at 14 °C. Fig. 7b showcases the correlation between normalized intensity and the studied temperatures. The intensity is normalized with the PL intensity value at 14 °C. The decrease is approximately linear with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99538. The fitted line has an equation I = −0.00658 (T) + 1.10372. The slope of the line directly gives the thermal sensitivity of the sensor, which assesses how effectively and accurately a sensor or instrument can detect temperature changes. In this case, the value is 0.00658 i.e. 0.658 % °C-1. It signifies that for every degree Celsius change in temperature, the PL intensity changes by 0.658 %.
A comparison of similar temperature-based sensors is drawn in Table 1.

The approximate linear decrease in PL intensity was further validated by the plot of integrated PL intensity against temperature (Fig. 7c). This decrease could be attributed to changes in band gap energy, thermal expansion, thermal quenching, exciton-phonon interactions, and modifications in surface states and defects as the temperature rises [64,66,67,71]. Other critical parameters, such as λmax (the wavelength of maximum PL emission intensity) and FWHM (full width at half maximum), pertaining to the PL emission spectra at various temperatures were also analyzed. A consistent λmax value is vital for CQD-based temperature sensors to achieve precise, sensitive, stable, and reliable temperature readings. The λmax value (446±3 nm) shows minimal variation from 14 °C to 84 °C (Fig. 7d), indicating an almost negligible spectral shift and minimal band gap fluctuations. These findings differ from the typical behavior of most semiconductor nanocrystals, which have band gaps that get modified with temperature [72,73]. A notable redshift (11 nm) was observed at 98 °C compared to 84 °C. This could be due to changes in band gap energy, thermal expansion, and thermal quenching at high temperatures. Thermal broadening of the PL emission spectra at different temperatures was estimated with the FWHM value (Fig. 7e). FWHM value at 14 °C is 111 and with every 14 °C increase in temperature the value increases by 1 or 2 units. It is 119 for 98 °C. A minor increase in the FWHM value with the temperature rise was observed, suggesting slight thermal broadening with temperature. Repeatability and reversibility are essential qualities that ensure the reliability, accuracy, and durability of PL-based temperature sensors, enhancing their effectiveness across various applications. The PL intensity was measured at 19 °C and 79 °C over five consecutive heating and cooling cycles and the outcomes are presented in Fig. 7f. A slight variation in the intensity shows that the thermosensor retains good reversibility, demonstrating its ability to maintain approximately stable performance over multiple thermal transitions.

The stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the temperature-sensing probe were further systematically evaluated under varying experimental conditions. For stability assessment, the probe is irradiated under a UV lamp (254 nm) and CFL light at 28 °C and 70 °C for three hours independently, with PL intensity measured every 30 min. Under UV light (Fig. 8 a), the intensity remained nearly constant, with minor fluctuation between the initial and final values and very low Relative standard deviation (RSD %) of 0.33 % and 1.7 % for 28 °C and 70 °C, respectively, suggesting the stable behavior of the probe under UV light. Under CFL irradiation (Fig. 8b), the changes in the PL intensity are marginal at 28 °C with an RSD % of 1.004 %, while at 70 °C the intensity remains approximately 86 % of the initial value with an RSD % of 5.94 %. The results also confirm stability under CFL light.

Furthermore, the repeatability of the sensing probe was assessed by performing seven consecutive PL measurements at 28 °C and 70 °C (Fig. 8c). The intensity remains consistent at both temperatures with a very low RSD % of 0.464 and 0.563 % for 28 °C and 70 °C, respectively. For stability with storage time, samples were stored for seven days in dark conditions and the PL intensity was recorded every day at 28 °C and 70 °C (Fig. 8d). The results are consistent at both temperatures with RSD % of 0.265 and 0.30 % for 28 °C and 70 °C, respectively. Overall, the findings demonstrate that the temperature-sensing probe exhibits good stability under UV and CFL irradiation, along with high repeatability and reproducibility. These attributes establish it as a reliable candidate for temperature sensing applications.

Metal ion detection is critical because of its significant impact on health, the environment, and industry. CQDs provide a powerful and adaptable basis for developing sensitive, selective, and cost-effective metal ion sensors, making them vital in today's analytical and monitoring applications. The presently synthesized CQDs have been also utilized for metal ions detection in aqueous solutions. Ca2+, Ag2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Pb2+, K+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Ti4+, Zn2+ and, As5+ ions were used for testing the selectivity of CQDs. The stock solution of metal ions was prepared in DI with an ion concentration of 5 mM. In a typical experiment, 200 µL of CQDs were added to 2.5 ml of the prepared stock solutions and left for 5 min. The same process was repeated with all the ion solutions. The PL responses were recorded at 371 nm excitation wavelength under normal temperature and pressure conditions. The obtained intensity values were normalized (F/F0, where F = PL intensity in various metal ions solution, and F0 = PL intensity in blank sample) to the intensity of the blank sample (200 µL of CQDs + 2.5 ml DI). Fig. 9 a illustrates the results. In the majority of the metal ion solutions, PL intensity remained practically unaltered, while in others, considerable change was detected. Altogether, the maximum PL intensity decreased in the case of Cr6+ ions and remained at approximately 0.01 % of the blank samples PL intensity. The findings imply that, in contrast to other metal ions, CQDs are highly sensitive to Cr6+ ions. The interference of most interfering, medium interfering ions like Ca2+, Ag2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Co2+ and their combinations such as Cu2++ Ag2++ Co2+, Cu2++ Ag2++ Ca2+, Cu2++ Ag2++ Sn2+, and Co2++ Sn2++ Ca2+ with Cr6+ions is being investigated. The results (Fig. 10 a) show minimal changes in normalized PL intensity (F/F0) when compared to Cr6+ions, indicating negligible interference. This suggests that CQDs are selective to Cr6+ ions having negligible interference with other ions.

Fig. 9b shows the PL responses of CQDs in various concentrations (0–500 µM) of Cr6+ ions. The normalized intensity (F/F0) was plotted against various concentrations (0–500 µM) of Cr6+ ions in Fig. 9c. The intensity decreases as the concentration of Cr6+ ions increases and remains approximately 0.07 % of the initial value at 500 µM. The decrease in the PL intensity is found to be linear in a range of 0–40 µM (Fig. 9d), this region is used to approximate the LOD. It is approximated using the following formula
L O D = 3
σ k
, where σ is the standard error of intercept, and k is the slope of the linear regression region [74]. The equation of the fitted line is
y = − 0.0087 x + 0.994
with R2 = 0.96. where slope (k) is 0.0087, the y-intercept is 0.994 with a standard error in intercept 0.02412. The determined LOD value is 8.3 µM, indicating that the sensor can detect Cr6+ ions in solutions at concentrations as low as 8.3 micromolar (μM) and higher.

Furthermore, the repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the metal ions sensor were also investigated. For these tests, we randomly choose two concentrations (100 and 200 µM) of Cr6+ ions in DI water. The tests were performed with 200 µL CQDs in 2.5 mL respective solutions and the number of taken measurements was seven. For repeatability, the PL intensity of the same samples with 100 and 200 µM concentrations of Cr6+ was measured 7 times and the normalized PL intensity is plotted in Fig. 10b. All the measurements were approximately consistent with relative standard deviation (RSD)% of 2.35 and 4.04 % for 100 and 200 µM respectively, indicating good repeatability.

Seven independently prepared samples of 100 and 200 µM Cr6+ concentrations were tested for reproducibility (Fig. 10c). The results are approximately the same with very low RSD% of 1.91 and 0.85 %, suggesting that the sensor gives reliable results across different sample preparations. In addition, the stability of the sensor was evaluated by monitoring the PL responses of the samples stored for a week with the PL intensity measured every day. The samples were stored at room temperature in dark conditions. The results are approximately consistent (Fig. 10d), with very low RSD% of 0.91 and 1.99 % for 100 and 200 µM Cr6+ concentrations, respectively. All the aforementioned results suggest that the sensor has very little interference with other competing metal ions, and possesses repeatability, reproducibility, and stability over an extended period. These properties make it a good candidate for practical applications.

The practicality of the present sensor is tested in the two real water samples, tap water (taken from the institute laboratory) and groundwater (from the borewell near the institute campus) by using the well-known standard addition method [75–77]. Both samples were filtered with a 0.22 µm syringe filter and diluted with DI water. Since no Cr6+ ions were detected in the pure samples, the samples were spiked with a known concentration (10, 20, and 30 µM) of Cr6+ ions. The probe's performance was assessed by recovered concentration from the samples calculated from the calibration curve (Fig. 9d). In the tap water, the probe successfully recovered 8.5, 21, and 25 µM for 10, 20, and 30 µM with very low RSD % of 0.86, 3.30, and 2.02 % respectively. In groundwater, the probe recovered 11.9, 21.1, and 27 µM for 10, 20, and 30 µM spiked concentrations with RSD % of 0.77, 1.69, and 1.18 % respectively. The related data is summarized in Table 2 , where n represents the number of measurements. All the aforementioned results point toward the precision and reliability of the present probe to detect Cr6+ ions in real water samples, making it suitable for practical applications.

A comparison of Cr6+ sensing probes based on the PL quenching mechanism of CQDs is provided in Table 3 . The LOD of the current sensor for Cr⁶⁺ ions is well within the typical concentration range of Cr⁶⁺ found in environmental water samples, making it suitable for real-world applications. In addition, its repeatability, reproducibility, stability, and feasibility in real water samples establish it as a valuable tool for the detection of Cr6+ ions in environmental water systems.

The decrease in the PL intensity of a fluorophore due to the interaction with some other ion or molecule is called fluorescence quenching. Broadly, it is of two types: static and dynamic (collisional) quenching. The process of static quenching entails the ground-state formation of a non-emissive complex between the fluorophore and quencher leading to PL intensity quenching. During dynamic quenching, fluorescence loss is attributed to the excited-state collisions between fluorophores and quenchers. Static and dynamic quenching involves various quenching mechanisms like non-fluorescent complex formation, FRET, IFE, PET, etc.

Stern-Volmer plots are valuable tools for differentiating between static and dynamic quenching. They provide insight into quenching mechanisms based on the effect of quencher concentrations on PL intensity. The relationship between fluorescence intensity with quencher concentrations is demonstrated by the stern Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k
s v
[ Q ]
Where, F0 = PL intensity without the presence of a quencher, F = PL intensity in the presence of a quencher, [Q] = quencher concentrations, and ksv = stern-Volmer quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant ksv is a key factor in differentiating between static and dynamic quenching. For static quenching ksv decreases with the rise in temperature and the opposite is the case in dynamic quenching. The effect of quencher (Cr6+) concentrations on the PL intensity at temperatures (30, 50, 70, and 90 °C) was studied and their corresponding Stern-Volmer plots are presented in Fig. 11 a. The Stern-Volmer equation, ksv, and R2 related to each temperature are summarized in Table 4 .

The ksv value first increases with the temperature from 30 to 70 °C and then decreases at 90 °C. That is, at lower and moderate temperature ranges (30–70 °C) dynamic quenching dominates while at high temperatures (90 °C) the contribution from static quenching decreases as the ground state complexes can be disrupted by thermal energy. The overall behavior is thus an interplay of both static and dynamic quenching [84–88]. The quenching mechanism is further elucidated by comparing the UV-visible spectra of CQDs, Cr6+ ions, and the mixture of CQDs and Cr6+ ions. As presented in Fig. 11b the UV-visible spectra of Cr6+ ions show peaks at about 272.5 nm and 372 nm, which remain approximately unchanged (no peak shift, no peak broadening, no appearance of new peaks) except the decrease in the absorbance intensity after CQDs addition. This lack of change in the spectra suggests that the addition of CQDs does not significantly alter the electronic environment of Cr6+ ions pointing towards the weak coordination between Cr6+ and CQDs. The decrease in the absorbance intensity indicates the quenching effect primarily due to dynamic quenching which could be due to processes like FRET, PET, IFE, etc. For FRET, the spectral overlap of emission spectra of CQDs and absorption spectra of Cr6+ ions is one of the necessary conditions. Fig. 11c compares the emission spectra of CQDs with the absorbance spectra of Cr6+ ions. In the 350–450 nm region their spectra overlap, which marked the likelihood of energy transfer from CQDs to Cr6+ ions through the FRET process and leads to their PL intensity quenching. From Fig. 5b (excitation/emission spectra of CQDs), the PL excitation and emission spectra of CQDs also overlap which suggests the possibility of IFE is also one of the contributing factors in the quenching process [89–91]. To further elucidate the quenching mechanism, we analyzed the FTIR spectra of pure CQDs in DI water, Cr6+ ions in DI water, and a Cr6+/CQDs mixture, as shown in Fig. 11d. The comparison reveals that the transmittance peak intensities of CQDs decrease upon the addition of Cr6+ ions, particularly in the OH/NH, C=O, and CO stretching regions. The reduction in peak intensities for the OH/NH, C=O, and CO stretches suggests that the presence of Cr6+ions has altered the vibrational dynamics of these functional groups. It can be due to several possible reasons like complex formation between Cr6+ ions and CQDs, electron transfer from CQDs to Cr6+ ions, or surface modification of CQDs which ultimately influences their PL behavior [92–94]. PL lifetime measurement serves as a key technique to differentiate static from dynamic quenching. In static quenching, a ground-state process forms a non-emissive complex, typically leaving the PL lifetime of the CQDs unchanged after the inclusion of Cr6+ ions. Conversely, dynamic quenching, which involves interactions in the excited state, often reduces the PL lifetime. For pure CQDs, the average PL lifetime is 0.0445 ns, and after adding Cr6+ ions, it changes marginally to 0.0454 ns (Fig. 11e). This minor shift of only 0.0009 ns suggests that the quenching process is primarily static [38,92,95–98]. Zeta potential analysis of CQDs provides information on their surface charge which in turn can influence their interactions with Cr6+ ions. The zeta potential for CQDs is −12.20 mV, while after the addition of Cr6+ ions, it reduces to −5.87 mV (Fig. 11f). The results point towards the adsorption of Cr6+ ions on the CQDs surface, highlighting the electrostatic interactions and surface complexation between CQDs and CR6+ ions, which eventually leads to the quenching of PL intensity through the static quenching process [37,99,100]. All the aforementioned results suggest that the static type of quenching process is more prominent in this system. A schematic illustration showing the PL quenching of CQDs by Cr6+ ions is shown in Fig. 12 .

In this study, cumin seeds are utilized for CQDs preparation using the hydrothermal technique of nanoparticle synthesis. It is a single-step process that uses no harsh chemicals and turns a kitchen spice i.e. cumin into a valuable optical material. The process is economical and has no adverse effect on the environment. The obtained CQDs have a nearly spherical morphology, non-uniform size distribution typically in the range of 0–8 nm with an average of 3.4 nm, primary composition of carbon, fluorine, oxygen, and nitrogen, presence of various hydrophilic chemical moieties on the surface, remarkable photostability, excellent optical properties. The sensitivity of PL intensity to both temperature and metal ions motivates us to utilize them for optical thermometry and ion-sensing applications. Their PL intensity decreases approximately linearly with the rise in temperature from 14 to 98 °C. The equation of the linear fitted line is I = −0.00658 (T) + 1.10372 with R2 = 0.99538. the thermal sensitivity value of the current temperature sensor is 0.00658 i.e. 0.658 % °C-1. Additionally, they were found selective and sensitive to Cr6+ ions among other tested metal ions. The PL intensity quenches significantly after the interaction of CQDs and Cr6+ions. The decrease in the PL intensity with the increasing concentrations of Cr6+ ions was observed. This decrease is approximately linear in the region of 0–40 µM. The equation of the linearly fitted line is
y = − 0.0087 x + 0.994
with R2 = 0.96. The lowest LOD value calculated from this linear region is 8.3 µM. In a nutshell, this study highlights the successful preparation of CQDs from cumin seeds and their application in metal ion detection and temperature sensing. The findings not only showcase the potential of these CQDs for practical sensing applications but also pave the way for broader utilization in areas such as bioimaging, photocatalysis, and optoelectronic devices. Additionally, the eco-friendly and cost-effective nature of the precursor suggests a sustainable approach to CQD synthesis, opening new avenues for further exploration in environmental monitoring, energy conversion, and biomedical applications. This work provides a strong foundation for future innovations in multifunctional sensing technologies and beyond.
